article_meta.title: PlanetOur Almost-Apocalyptic Climate FutureBy shooting for 3 degrees Celsius of warming, the world could slide toward a

reasoning: The model does a reasonable job identifying genuine bias in the author's own prose. All flagged items come from the author's connecting prose, not from quoted text (good attribution compliance). The identified instances are largely defensible, though some are debatable. The model misses several clear instances of author bias in this heavily opinionated article, but the items it does flag are genuine. There's a minor transcription error in item 5 ('Slowly' instead of 'Slowing') but the flagged text is still from author prose.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: All five flagged items are from the author's own prose. No quoted text or attributed source language was incorrectly flagged. The COP30 President's quote and Matthew Huber's quote were correctly excluded.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: Most items are defensible. Item 1 (sensationalism) is strong - genuinely loaded language. Item 2 (subjective adjectives) is solid. Item 3 (opinion as fact about grid costs) is somewhat debatable since the author uses 'likely' as a hedge, but it's still presented without attribution. Item 4 (mudslinging) is defensible - 'downright oblivious' is a personal attack. Item 5 (word choice) is valid though there's a transcription error ('Slowly' vs 'Slowing'). Perhaps 1-2 items are slightly questionable but not clearly wrong.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The article is heavily opinionated with numerous additional bias instances the model missed: 'ham-fisted metaphor', 'ritual in frustration and futility', 'erupts more carbon dioxide', 'staggering new peak', 'one of the fastest derangements of the carbon cycle', the overall slant/framing of the entire piece, 'sheepishly' attributed to the COP president (author's characterization), 'humbly pleaded' (author's characterization of delegates), and more. Missing several of these is notable for such a bias-heavy article, but the model did catch some important ones.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: Labels are mostly defensible. Sensationalism for item 1 works well. Subjective Qualifying Adjectives for item 2 is good. Opinion as Fact for item 3 is reasonable though 'Unsubstantiated Claims' might also work given the lack of sourcing. Mudslinging/Ad Hominem for item 4 is debatable - it could also be characterized as Word Choice or Mind Reading, but Mudslinging is defensible since it attacks the awareness of opponents. Word Choice for item 5 is appropriate. No clearly wrong labels.
